name: num_nonnulls ( VARIADIC "any" ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of non-null arguments.

name: num_nulls ( VARIADIC "any" ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of null arguments.

name: abs ( numeric_type ) → numeric_type
describe: Absolute value

name: cbrt ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Cube root

name:
ceil ( numeric ) → numeric
ceil ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Nearest integer greater than or equal to argument

name:
ceiling ( numeric ) → numeric
ceiling ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Nearest integer greater than or equal to argument (same as ceil )

name: degrees ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Converts radians to degrees

name: div ( y numeric , x numeric ) → numeric
describe: Integer quotient of y / x (truncates towards zero)

name: erf ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Error function

name: erfc ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Complementary error function ( 1 - erf(x) , without loss of precision for large inputs)

name:
exp ( numeric ) → numeric
exp ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Exponential ( e raised to the given power)

name: factorial ( bigint ) → numeric
describe: Factorial

name:
floor ( numeric ) → numeric
floor ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Nearest integer less than or equal to argument

name: gcd ( numeric_type , numeric_type ) → numeric_type
describe: Greatest common divisor (the largest positive number that divides both inputs with no remainder); returns 0 if both inputs are zero; available for integer , bigint , and numeric

name: lcm ( numeric_type , numeric_type ) → numeric_type
describe: Least common multiple (the smallest strictly positive number that is an integral multiple of both inputs); returns 0 if either input is zero; available for integer , bigint , and numeric

name:
ln ( numeric ) → numeric
ln ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Natural logarithm

name:
log ( numeric ) → numeric
log ( double precision ) → double precision
log ( b numeric , x numeric ) → numeric
describe: Base 10 logarithm Logarithm of x to base b

name:
log10 ( numeric ) → numeric
log10 ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Base 10 logarithm (same as log )

name: min_scale ( numeric ) → integer
describe: Minimum scale (number of fractional decimal digits) needed to represent the supplied value precisely

name: mod ( y numeric_type , x numeric_type ) → numeric_type
describe: Remainder of y / x ; available for smallint , integer , bigint , and numeric

name: pi ( ) → double precision
describe: Approximate value of π

name:
power ( a numeric , b numeric ) → numeric
power ( a double precision , b double precision ) → double precision
describe: a raised to the power of b

name: radians ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Converts degrees to radians

name:
round ( numeric ) → numeric
round ( double precision ) → double precision
round ( v numeric , s integer ) → numeric
describe: Rounds to nearest integer. For numeric , ties are broken by rounding away from zero. For double precision , the tie-breaking behavior is platform dependent, but “ round to nearest even ” is the most common rule. Rounds v to s decimal places. Ties are broken by rounding away from zero.

name: scale ( numeric ) → integer
describe: Scale of the argument (the number of decimal digits in the fractional part)

name:
sign ( numeric ) → numeric
sign ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Sign of the argument (-1, 0, or +1)

name:
sqrt ( numeric ) → numeric
sqrt ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Square root

name: trim_scale ( numeric ) → numeric
describe: Reduces the value's scale (number of fractional decimal digits) by removing trailing zeroes

name:
trunc ( numeric ) → numeric
trunc ( double precision ) → double precision
trunc ( v numeric , s integer ) → numeric
describe: Truncates to integer (towards zero) Truncates v to s decimal places

name:
width_bucket ( operand numeric , low numeric , high numeric , count integer ) → integer
width_bucket ( operand double precision , low double precision , high double precision , count integer ) → integer
width_bucket ( operand anycompatible , thresholds anycompatiblearray ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of the bucket in which operand falls in a histogram having count equal-width buckets spanning the range low to high . Returns 0 or count +1 for an input outside that range. Returns the number of the bucket in which operand falls given an array listing the lower bounds of the buckets. Returns 0 for an input less than the first lower bound. operand and the array elements can be of any type having standard comparison operators. The thresholds array must be sorted , smallest first, or unexpected results will be obtained.

name:
random ( ) → double precision
random ( min integer , max integer ) → integer
random ( min bigint , max bigint ) → bigint
random ( min numeric , max numeric ) → numeric
describe: Returns a random value in the range 0.0 <= x < 1.0 Returns a random value in the range min <= x <= max . For type numeric , the result will have the same number of fractional decimal digits as min or max , whichever has more.

name: random_normal ( [ mean double precision [ , stddev double precision ] ] ) → double precision
describe: Returns a random value from the normal distribution with the given parameters; mean defaults to 0.0 and stddev defaults to 1.0

name: setseed ( double precision ) → void
describe: Sets the seed for subsequent random() and random_normal() calls; argument must be between -1.0 and 1.0, inclusive setseed(0.12345)

name: acos ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse cosine, result in radians

name: acosd ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse cosine, result in degrees

name: asin ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse sine, result in radians

name: asind ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse sine, result in degrees

name: atan ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse tangent, result in radians

name: atand ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse tangent, result in degrees

name: atan2 ( y double precision , x double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse tangent of y / x , result in radians

name: atan2d ( y double precision , x double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse tangent of y / x , result in degrees

name: cos ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Cosine, argument in radians

name: cosd ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Cosine, argument in degrees

name: cot ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Cotangent, argument in radians

name: cotd ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Cotangent, argument in degrees

name: sin ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Sine, argument in radians

name: sind ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Sine, argument in degrees

name: tan ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Tangent, argument in radians

name: tand ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Tangent, argument in degrees

name: sinh ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Hyperbolic sine

name: cosh ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Hyperbolic cosine

name: tanh ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Hyperbolic tangent

name: asinh ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse hyperbolic sine

name: acosh ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse hyperbolic cosine

name: atanh ( double precision ) → double precision
describe: Inverse hyperbolic tangent

name: btrim ( string text [ , characters text ] ) → text
describe: Removes the longest string containing only characters in characters (a space by default) from the start and end of string .

name: bit_length ( text ) → integer
describe: Returns number of bits in the string (8 times the octet_length ).

name:
char_length ( text ) → integer
character_length ( text ) → integer
describe: Returns number of characters in the string.

name: lower ( text ) → text
describe: Converts the string to all lower case, according to the rules of the database's locale.

name: lpad ( string text , length integer [ , fill text ] ) → text
describe: Extends the string to length length by prepending the characters fill (a space by default). If the string is already longer than length then it is truncated (on the right).

name: ltrim ( string text [ , characters text ] ) → text
describe: Removes the longest string containing only characters in characters (a space by default) from the start of string .

name: normalize ( text [ , form ] ) → text
describe: Converts the string to the specified Unicode normalization form. The optional form key word specifies the form: NFC (the default), NFD , NFKC , or NFKD . This function can only be used when the server encoding is UTF8 .

name:
octet_length ( text ) → integer
octet_length ( character ) → integer
describe: Returns number of bytes in the string. Returns number of bytes in the string. Since this version of the function accepts type character directly, it will not strip trailing spaces.
example:
octet_length('josé') → 5 (if server encoding is UTF8)
octet_length('abc '::character(4)) → 4

name: overlay ( string text PLACING newsubstring text FROM start integer [ FOR count integer ] ) → text
describe: Replaces the substring of string that starts at the start 'th character and extends for count characters with newsubstring . If count is omitted, it defaults to the length of newsubstring .

name: position ( substring text IN string text ) → integer
describe: Returns first starting index of the specified substring within string , or zero if it's not present.

name: rpad ( string text , length integer [ , fill text ] ) → text
describe: Extends the string to length length by appending the characters fill (a space by default). If the string is already longer than length then it is truncated.

name: rtrim ( string text [ , characters text ] ) → text
describe: Removes the longest string containing only characters in characters (a space by default) from the end of string .

name:
substring ( string text [ FROM start integer ] [ FOR count integer ] ) → text
substring ( string text FROM pattern text ) → text
substring ( string text SIMILAR pattern text ESCAPE escape text ) → text
substring ( string text FROM pattern text FOR escape text ) → text
describe: Extracts the substring of string starting at the start 'th character if that is specified, and stopping after count characters if that is specified. Provide at least one of start and count . Extracts the first substring matching POSIX regular expression; see Section 9.7.3 . Extracts the first substring matching SQL regular expression; see Section 9.7.2 . The first form has been specified since SQL:2003; the second form was only in SQL:1999 and should be considered obsolete.
example:
substring('Thomas' from 2 for 3) → hom
substring('Thomas' from 3) → omas
substring('Thomas' for 2) → Th
substring('Thomas' from '...$') → mas
substring('Thomas' similar '%#"o_a#"_' escape '#') → oma

name:
trim ( [ LEADING | TRAILING | BOTH ] [ characters text ] FROM string text ) → text
trim ( [ LEADING | TRAILING | BOTH ] [ FROM ] string text [ , characters text ] ) → text
describe: Removes the longest string containing only characters in characters (a space by default) from the start, end, or both ends ( BOTH is the default) of string . This is a non-standard syntax for trim() .
example:
trim(both 'xyz' from 'yxTomxx') → Tom
trim(both from 'yxTomxx', 'xyz') → Tom

name: unicode_assigned ( text ) → boolean
describe: Returns true if all characters in the string are assigned Unicode codepoints; false otherwise. This function can only be used when the server encoding is UTF8 .

name: upper ( text ) → text
describe: Converts the string to all upper case, according to the rules of the database's locale.

name: ascii ( text ) → integer
describe: Returns the numeric code of the first character of the argument. In UTF8 encoding, returns the Unicode code point of the character. In other multibyte encodings, the argument must be an ASCII character.

name: chr ( integer ) → text
describe: Returns the character with the given code. In UTF8 encoding the argument is treated as a Unicode code point. In other multibyte encodings the argument must designate an ASCII character. chr(0) is disallowed because text data types cannot store that character.

name: concat ( val1 "any" [, val2 "any" [, ...] ] ) → text
describe: Concatenates the text representations of all the arguments. NULL arguments are ignored.

name: concat_ws ( sep text , val1 "any" [, val2 "any" [, ...] ] ) → text
describe: Concatenates all but the first argument, with separators. The first argument is used as the separator string, and should not be NULL. Other NULL arguments are ignored.

name: format ( formatstr text [, formatarg "any" [, ...] ] ) → text
describe: Formats arguments according to a format string; see Section 9.4.1 . This function is similar to the C function sprintf .

name: initcap ( text ) → text
describe: Converts the first letter of each word to upper case and the rest to lower case. Words are sequences of alphanumeric characters separated by non-alphanumeric characters.

name: left ( string text , n integer ) → text
describe: Returns first n characters in the string, or when n is negative, returns all but last | n | characters.

name: length ( text ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of characters in the string.

name: md5 ( text ) → text
describe: Computes the MD5 hash of the argument, with the result written in hexadecimal.

name: parse_ident ( qualified_identifier text [, strict_mode boolean DEFAULT true ] ) → text[]
describe: Splits qualified_identifier into an array of identifiers, removing any quoting of individual identifiers. By default, extra characters after the last identifier are considered an error; but if the second parameter is false , then such extra characters are ignored. (This behavior is useful for parsing names for objects like functions.) Note that this function does not truncate over-length identifiers. If you want truncation you can cast the result to name[] .

name: pg_client_encoding ( ) → name
describe: Returns current client encoding name.

name: quote_ident ( text ) → text
describe: Returns the given string suitably quoted to be used as an identifier in an SQL statement string. Quotes are added only if necessary (i.e., if the string contains non-identifier characters or would be case-folded). Embedded quotes are properly doubled. See also Example 41.1 .

name:
quote_literal ( text ) → text
quote_literal ( anyelement ) → text
describe: Returns the given string suitably quoted to be used as a string literal in an SQL statement string. Embedded single-quotes and backslashes are properly doubled. Note that quote_literal returns null on null input; if the argument might be null, quote_nullable is often more suitable. See also Example 41.1 . Converts the given value to text and then quotes it as a literal. Embedded single-quotes and backslashes are properly doubled.

name:
quote_nullable ( text ) → text
quote_nullable ( anyelement ) → text
describe: Returns the given string suitably quoted to be used as a string literal in an SQL statement string; or, if the argument is null, returns NULL . Embedded single-quotes and backslashes are properly doubled. See also Example 41.1 . Converts the given value to text and then quotes it as a literal; or, if the argument is null, returns NULL . Embedded single-quotes and backslashes are properly doubled.

name: regexp_count ( string text , pattern text [, start integer [, flags text ] ] ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of times the POSIX regular expression pattern matches in the string ; see Section 9.7.3 .

name: regexp_instr ( string text , pattern text [, start integer [, N integer [, endoption integer [, flags text [, subexpr integer ] ] ] ] ] ) → integer
describe: Returns the position within string where the N 'th match of the POSIX regular expression pattern occurs, or zero if there is no such match; see Section 9.7.3 .

name: regexp_like ( string text , pattern text [, flags text ] ) → boolean
describe: Checks whether a match of the POSIX regular expression pattern occurs within string ; see Section 9.7.3 .

name: regexp_match ( string text , pattern text [, flags text ] ) → text[]
describe: Returns substrings within the first match of the POSIX regular expression pattern to the string ; see Section 9.7.3 .

name: regexp_matches ( string text , pattern text [, flags text ] ) → setof text[]
describe: Returns substrings within the first match of the POSIX regular expression pattern to the string , or substrings within all such matches if the g flag is used; see Section 9.7.3 .

name:
regexp_replace ( string text , pattern text , replacement text [, start integer ] [, flags text ] ) → text
regexp_replace ( string text , pattern text , replacement text , start integer , N integer [, flags text ] ) → text
describe: Replaces the substring that is the first match to the POSIX regular expression pattern , or all such matches if the g flag is used; see Section 9.7.3 . Replaces the substring that is the N 'th match to the POSIX regular expression pattern , or all such matches if N is zero; see Section 9.7.3 .

name: regexp_split_to_array ( string text , pattern text [, flags text ] ) → text[]
describe: Splits string using a POSIX regular expression as the delimiter, producing an array of results; see Section 9.7.3 .

name: regexp_split_to_table ( string text , pattern text [, flags text ] ) → setof text
describe: Splits string using a POSIX regular expression as the delimiter, producing a set of results; see Section 9.7.3 .

name: regexp_substr ( string text , pattern text [, start integer [, N integer [, flags text [, subexpr integer ] ] ] ] ) → text
describe: Returns the substring within string that matches the N 'th occurrence of the POSIX regular expression pattern , or NULL if there is no such match; see Section 9.7.3 .

name: repeat ( string text , number integer ) → text
describe: Repeats string the specified number of times.

name: replace ( string text , from text , to text ) → text
describe: Replaces all occurrences in string of substring from with substring to .

name: reverse ( text ) → text
describe: Reverses the order of the characters in the string.

name: right ( string text , n integer ) → text
describe: Returns last n characters in the string, or when n is negative, returns all but first | n | characters.

name: split_part ( string text , delimiter text , n integer ) → text
describe: Splits string at occurrences of delimiter and returns the n 'th field (counting from one), or when n is negative, returns the | n |'th-from-last field.

name: starts_with ( string text , prefix text ) → boolean
describe: Returns true if string starts with prefix .

name: string_to_array ( string text , delimiter text [ , null_string text ] ) → text[]
describe: Splits the string at occurrences of delimiter and forms the resulting fields into a text array. If delimiter is NULL , each character in the string will become a separate element in the array. If delimiter is an empty string, then the string is treated as a single field. If null_string is supplied and is not NULL , fields matching that string are replaced by NULL . See also array_to_string .

name: string_to_table ( string text , delimiter text [ , null_string text ] ) → setof text
describe: Splits the string at occurrences of delimiter and returns the resulting fields as a set of text rows. If delimiter is NULL , each character in the string will become a separate row of the result. If delimiter is an empty string, then the string is treated as a single field. If null_string is supplied and is not NULL , fields matching that string are replaced by NULL .

name: strpos ( string text , substring text ) → integer
describe: Returns first starting index of the specified substring within string , or zero if it's not present. (Same as position( substring in string ) , but note the reversed argument order.)

name: substr ( string text , start integer [ , count integer ] ) → text
describe: Extracts the substring of string starting at the start 'th character, and extending for count characters if that is specified. (Same as substring( string from start for count ) .)

name:
to_ascii ( string text ) → text
to_ascii ( string text , encoding name ) → text
to_ascii ( string text , encoding integer ) → text
describe: Converts string to ASCII from another encoding, which may be identified by name or number. If encoding is omitted the database encoding is assumed (which in practice is the only useful case). The conversion consists primarily of dropping accents. Conversion is only supported from LATIN1 , LATIN2 , LATIN9 , and WIN1250 encodings. (See the unaccent module for another, more flexible solution.)

name:
to_bin ( integer ) → text
to_bin ( bigint ) → text
describe: Converts the number to its equivalent two's complement binary representation.

name:
to_hex ( integer ) → text
to_hex ( bigint ) → text
describe: Converts the number to its equivalent two's complement hexadecimal representation.

name:
to_oct ( integer ) → text
to_oct ( bigint ) → text
describe: Converts the number to its equivalent two's complement octal representation.

name: translate ( string text , from text , to text ) → text
describe: Replaces each character in string that matches a character in the from set with the corresponding character in the to set. If from is longer than to , occurrences of the extra characters in from are deleted.

name: unistr ( text ) → text
describe: Evaluate escaped Unicode characters in the argument. Unicode characters can be specified as \ XXXX (4 hexadecimal digits), \+ XXXXXX (6 hexadecimal digits), \u XXXX (4 hexadecimal digits), or \U XXXXXXXX (8 hexadecimal digits). To specify a backslash, write two backslashes. All other characters are taken literally. If the server encoding is not UTF-8, the Unicode code point identified by one of these escape sequences is converted to the actual server encoding; an error is reported if that's not possible. This function provides a (non-standard) alternative to string constants with Unicode escapes (see Section 4.1.2.3 ).

name: bit_length ( bytea ) → integer
describe: Returns number of bits in the binary string (8 times the octet_length ).

name: btrim ( bytes bytea , bytesremoved bytea ) → bytea
describe: Removes the longest string containing only bytes appearing in bytesremoved from the start and end of bytes .

name: ltrim ( bytes bytea , bytesremoved bytea ) → bytea
describe: Removes the longest string containing only bytes appearing in bytesremoved from the start of bytes .

name: octet_length ( bytea ) → integer
describe: Returns number of bytes in the binary string.

name: overlay ( bytes bytea PLACING newsubstring bytea FROM start integer [ FOR count integer ] ) → bytea
describe: Replaces the substring of bytes that starts at the start 'th byte and extends for count bytes with newsubstring . If count is omitted, it defaults to the length of newsubstring .

name: position ( substring bytea IN bytes bytea ) → integer
describe: Returns first starting index of the specified substring within bytes , or zero if it's not present.

name: rtrim ( bytes bytea , bytesremoved bytea ) → bytea
describe: Removes the longest string containing only bytes appearing in bytesremoved from the end of bytes .

name: substring ( bytes bytea [ FROM start integer ] [ FOR count integer ] ) → bytea
describe: Extracts the substring of bytes starting at the start 'th byte if that is specified, and stopping after count bytes if that is specified. Provide at least one of start and count .

name:
trim ( [ LEADING | TRAILING | BOTH ] bytesremoved bytea FROM bytes bytea ) → bytea
trim ( [ LEADING | TRAILING | BOTH ] [ FROM ] bytes bytea , bytesremoved bytea ) → bytea
describe: Removes the longest string containing only bytes appearing in bytesremoved from the start, end, or both ends ( BOTH is the default) of bytes . This is a non-standard syntax for trim() .

name: bit_count ( bytes bytea ) → bigint
describe: Returns the number of bits set in the binary string (also known as “ popcount ” ).

name: get_bit ( bytes bytea , n bigint ) → integer
describe: Extracts n'th bit from binary string.

name: get_byte ( bytes bytea , n integer ) → integer
describe: Extracts n'th byte from binary string.

name:
length ( bytea ) → integer
length ( bytes bytea , encoding name ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of bytes in the binary string. Returns the number of characters in the binary string, assuming that it is text in the given encoding .

name: md5 ( bytea ) → text
describe: Computes the MD5 hash of the binary string, with the result written in hexadecimal.

name: set_bit ( bytes bytea , n bigint , newvalue integer ) → bytea
describe: Sets n'th bit in binary string to newvalue .

name: set_byte ( bytes bytea , n integer , newvalue integer ) → bytea
describe: Sets n'th byte in binary string to newvalue .

name: sha224 ( bytea ) → bytea
describe: Computes the SHA-224 hash of the binary string.

name: sha256 ( bytea ) → bytea
describe: Computes the SHA-256 hash of the binary string.

name: sha384 ( bytea ) → bytea
describe: Computes the SHA-384 hash of the binary string.

name: sha512 ( bytea ) → bytea
describe: Computes the SHA-512 hash of the binary string.

name: substr ( bytes bytea , start integer [ , count integer ] ) → bytea
describe: Extracts the substring of bytes starting at the start 'th byte, and extending for count bytes if that is specified. (Same as substring( bytes from start for count ) .)

name: convert ( bytes bytea , src_encoding name , dest_encoding name ) → bytea
describe: Converts a binary string representing text in encoding src_encoding to a binary string in encoding dest_encoding (see Section 23.3.4 for available conversions).

name: convert_from ( bytes bytea , src_encoding name ) → text
describe: Converts a binary string representing text in encoding src_encoding to text in the database encoding (see Section 23.3.4 for available conversions).

name: convert_to ( string text , dest_encoding name ) → bytea
describe: Converts a text string (in the database encoding) to a binary string encoded in encoding dest_encoding (see Section 23.3.4 for available conversions).

name: encode ( bytes bytea , format text ) → text
describe: Encodes binary data into a textual representation; supported format values are: base64 , escape , hex .

name: decode ( string text , format text ) → bytea
describe: Decodes binary data from a textual representation; supported format values are the same as for encode .

name: bit_count ( bit ) → bigint
describe: Returns the number of bits set in the bit string (also known as “ popcount ” ).

name: bit_length ( bit ) → integer
describe: Returns number of bits in the bit string.

name: length ( bit ) → integer
describe: Returns number of bits in the bit string.

name: octet_length ( bit ) → integer
describe: Returns number of bytes in the bit string.

name: overlay ( bits bit PLACING newsubstring bit FROM start integer [ FOR count integer ] ) → bit
describe: Replaces the substring of bits that starts at the start 'th bit and extends for count bits with newsubstring . If count is omitted, it defaults to the length of newsubstring .

name: position ( substring bit IN bits bit ) → integer
describe: Returns first starting index of the specified substring within bits , or zero if it's not present.

name: substring ( bits bit [ FROM start integer ] [ FOR count integer ] ) → bit
describe: Extracts the substring of bits starting at the start 'th bit if that is specified, and stopping after count bits if that is specified. Provide at least one of start and count .

name: get_bit ( bits bit , n integer ) → integer
describe: Extracts n 'th bit from bit string; the first (leftmost) bit is bit 0.

name: set_bit ( bits bit , n integer , newvalue integer ) → bit
describe: Sets n 'th bit in bit string to newvalue ; the first (leftmost) bit is bit 0.

name:
to_char ( timestamp , text ) → text
to_char ( timestamp with time zone , text ) → text
to_char ( interval , text ) → text
to_char ( numeric_type , text ) → text
describe: Converts time stamp to string according to the given format. Converts interval to string according to the given format. Converts number to string according to the given format; available for integer , bigint , numeric , real , double precision .

name: to_date ( text , text ) → date
describe: Converts string to date according to the given format.

name: to_number ( text , text ) → numeric
describe: Converts string to numeric according to the given format.

name: to_timestamp ( text , text ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Converts string to time stamp according to the given format. (See also to_timestamp(double precision) in Table 9.33 .)

name:
age ( timestamp , timestamp ) → interval
age ( timestamp ) → interval
describe: Subtract arguments, producing a “ symbolic ” result that uses years and months, rather than just days Subtract argument from current_date (at midnight)
example:
age(timestamp '2001-04-10', timestamp '1957-06-13') → 43 years 9 mons 27 days
age(timestamp '1957-06-13') → 62 years 6 mons 10 days

name: clock_timestamp ( ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Current date and time (changes during statement execution); see Section 9.9.5

name: current_time ( integer ) → time with time zone
describe: Current time of day, with limited precision; see Section 9.9.5

name: current_timestamp ( integer ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Current date and time (start of current transaction), with limited precision; see Section 9.9.5

name: date_add ( timestamp with time zone , interval [ , text ] ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Add an interval to a timestamp with time zone , computing times of day and daylight-savings adjustments according to the time zone named by the third argument, or the current TimeZone setting if that is omitted. The form with two arguments is equivalent to the timestamp with time zone + interval operator.

name: date_bin ( interval , timestamp , timestamp ) → timestamp
describe: Bin input into specified interval aligned with specified origin; see Section 9.9.3

name:
date_part ( text , timestamp ) → double precision
date_part ( text , interval ) → double precision
describe: Get timestamp subfield (equivalent to extract ); see Section 9.9.1 Get interval subfield (equivalent to extract ); see Section 9.9.1
example:
date_part('hour', timestamp '2001-02-16 20:38:40') → 20
date_part('month', interval '2 years 3 months') → 3

name: date_subtract ( timestamp with time zone , interval [ , text ] ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Subtract an interval from a timestamp with time zone , computing times of day and daylight-savings adjustments according to the time zone named by the third argument, or the current TimeZone setting if that is omitted. The form with two arguments is equivalent to the timestamp with time zone - interval operator.

name:
date_trunc ( text , timestamp ) → timestamp
date_trunc ( text , timestamp with time zone , text ) → timestamp with time zone
date_trunc ( text , interval ) → interval
describe: Truncate to specified precision; see Section 9.9.2 Truncate to specified precision in the specified time zone; see Section 9.9.2 Truncate to specified precision; see Section 9.9.2
example:
date_trunc('hour', timestamp '2001-02-16 20:38:40') → 2001-02-16 20:00:00
date_trunc('day', timestamptz '2001-02-16 20:38:40+00', 'Australia/Sydney') → 2001-02-16 13:00:00+00
date_trunc('hour', interval '2 days 3 hours 40 minutes') → 2 days 03:00:00

name:
extract ( field from timestamp ) → numeric
extract ( field from interval ) → numeric
describe: Get timestamp subfield; see Section 9.9.1 Get interval subfield; see Section 9.9.1
example:
extract(hour from timestamp '2001-02-16 20:38:40') → 20
extract(month from interval '2 years 3 months') → 3

name:
isfinite ( date ) → boolean
isfinite ( timestamp ) → boolean
isfinite ( interval ) → boolean
describe: Test for finite date (not +/-infinity) Test for finite timestamp (not +/-infinity) Test for finite interval (not +/-infinity)
example:
isfinite(date '2001-02-16') → true
isfinite(timestamp 'infinity') → false
isfinite(interval '4 hours') → true

name: justify_days ( interval ) → interval
describe: Adjust interval, converting 30-day time periods to months
example: justify_days(interval '1 year 65 days') → 1 year 2 mons 5 days

name: justify_hours ( interval ) → interval
describe: Adjust interval, converting 24-hour time periods to days

name: justify_interval ( interval ) → interval
describe: Adjust interval using justify_days and justify_hours , with additional sign adjustments

name: localtime ( integer ) → time
describe: Current time of day, with limited precision; see Section 9.9.5

name: localtimestamp ( integer ) → timestamp
describe: Current date and time (start of current transaction), with limited precision; see Section 9.9.5

name: make_date ( year int , month int , day int ) → date
describe: Create date from year, month and day fields (negative years signify BC)

name: make_interval ( [ years int [ , months int [ , weeks int [ , days int [ , hours int [ , mins int [ , secs double precision ] ] ] ] ] ] ] ) → interval
describe: Create interval from years, months, weeks, days, hours, minutes and seconds fields, each of which can default to zero

name: make_time ( hour int , min int , sec double precision ) → time
describe: Create time from hour, minute and seconds fields

name: make_timestamp ( year int , month int , day int , hour int , min int , sec double precision ) → timestamp
describe: Create timestamp from year, month, day, hour, minute and seconds fields (negative years signify BC)

name: make_timestamptz ( year int , month int , day int , hour int , min int , sec double precision [ , timezone text ] ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Create timestamp with time zone from year, month, day, hour, minute and seconds fields (negative years signify BC). If timezone is not specified, the current time zone is used; the examples assume the session time zone is Europe/London

name: now ( ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Current date and time (start of current transaction); see Section 9.9.5

name: statement_timestamp ( ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Current date and time (start of current statement); see Section 9.9.5

name: timeofday ( ) → text
describe: Current date and time (like clock_timestamp , but as a text string); see Section 9.9.5

name: transaction_timestamp ( ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Current date and time (start of current transaction); see Section 9.9.5

name: to_timestamp ( double precision ) → timestamp with time zone
describe: Convert Unix epoch (seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00+00) to timestamp with time zone

name: enum_first ( anyenum ) → anyenum
describe: Returns the first value of the input enum type.

name: enum_last ( anyenum ) → anyenum
describe: Returns the last value of the input enum type.

name:
enum_range ( anyenum ) → anyarray
enum_range ( anyenum , anyenum ) → anyarray
describe: Returns all values of the input enum type in an ordered array. Returns the range between the two given enum values, as an ordered array. The values must be from the same enum type. If the first parameter is null, the result will start with the first value of the enum type. If the second parameter is null, the result will end with the last value of the enum type.

name: area ( geometric_type ) → double precision
describe: Computes area. Available for box , path , circle . A path input must be closed, else NULL is returned. Also, if the path is self-intersecting, the result may be meaningless.

name: center ( geometric_type ) → point
describe: Computes center point. Available for box , circle .

name: diagonal ( box ) → lseg
describe: Extracts box's diagonal as a line segment (same as lseg(box) ).

name: diameter ( circle ) → double precision
describe: Computes diameter of circle.

name: height ( box ) → double precision
describe: Computes vertical size of box.

name: isclosed ( path ) → boolean
describe: Is path closed?

name: isopen ( path ) → boolean
describe: Is path open?

name: length ( geometric_type ) → double precision
describe: Computes the total length. Available for lseg , path .

name: npoints ( geometric_type ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of points. Available for path , polygon .

name: pclose ( path ) → path
describe: Converts path to closed form.

name: popen ( path ) → path
describe: Converts path to open form.

name: radius ( circle ) → double precision
describe: Computes radius of circle.

name: slope ( point , point ) → double precision
describe: Computes slope of a line drawn through the two points.

name: width ( box ) → double precision
describe: Computes horizontal size of box.

name:
box ( circle ) → box
box ( point ) → box
box ( point , point ) → box
box ( polygon ) → box
describe: Computes box inscribed within the circle. Converts point to empty box. Converts any two corner points to box. Computes bounding box of polygon.

name: bound_box ( box , box ) → box
describe: Computes bounding box of two boxes.

name:
circle ( box ) → circle
circle ( point , double precision ) → circle
circle ( polygon ) → circle
describe: Computes smallest circle enclosing box. Constructs circle from center and radius. Converts polygon to circle. The circle's center is the mean of the positions of the polygon's points, and the radius is the average distance of the polygon's points from that center.

name: line ( point , point ) → line
describe: Converts two points to the line through them.

name:
lseg ( box ) → lseg
lseg ( point , point ) → lseg
describe: Extracts box's diagonal as a line segment. Constructs line segment from two endpoints.

name: path ( polygon ) → path
describe: Converts polygon to a closed path with the same list of points.

name:
point ( double precision , double precision ) → point
point ( box ) → point
point ( circle ) → point
point ( lseg ) → point
point ( polygon ) → point
describe: Constructs point from its coordinates. Computes center of box. Computes center of circle. Computes center of line segment. Computes center of polygon (the mean of the positions of the polygon's points).

name:
polygon ( box ) → polygon
polygon ( circle ) → polygon
polygon ( integer , circle ) → polygon
polygon ( path ) → polygon
describe: Converts box to a 4-point polygon. Converts circle to a 12-point polygon. Converts circle to an n -point polygon. Converts closed path to a polygon with the same list of points.

name:
abbrev ( inet ) → text
abbrev ( cidr ) → text
describe: Creates an abbreviated display format as text. (The result is the same as the inet output function produces; it is “ abbreviated ” only in comparison to the result of an explicit cast to text , which for historical reasons will never suppress the netmask part.) Creates an abbreviated display format as text. (The abbreviation consists of dropping all-zero octets to the right of the netmask; more examples are in Table 8.22 .)

name: broadcast ( inet ) → inet
describe: Computes the broadcast address for the address's network.

name: family ( inet ) → integer
describe: Returns the address's family: 4 for IPv4, 6 for IPv6.

name: host ( inet ) → text
describe: Returns the IP address as text, ignoring the netmask.

name: hostmask ( inet ) → inet
describe: Computes the host mask for the address's network.

name: inet_merge ( inet , inet ) → cidr
describe: Computes the smallest network that includes both of the given networks.

name: inet_same_family ( inet , inet ) → boolean
describe: Tests whether the addresses belong to the same IP family.

name: masklen ( inet ) → integer
describe: Returns the netmask length in bits.

name: netmask ( inet ) → inet
describe: Computes the network mask for the address's network.

name: network ( inet ) → cidr
describe: Returns the network part of the address, zeroing out whatever is to the right of the netmask. (This is equivalent to casting the value to cidr .)

name:
set_masklen ( inet , integer ) → inet
set_masklen ( cidr , integer ) → cidr
describe: Sets the netmask length for an inet value. The address part does not change. Sets the netmask length for a cidr value. Address bits to the right of the new netmask are set to zero.

name: text ( inet ) → text
describe: Returns the unabbreviated IP address and netmask length as text. (This has the same result as an explicit cast to text .)

name:
trunc ( macaddr ) → macaddr
trunc ( macaddr8 ) → macaddr8
describe: Sets the last 3 bytes of the address to zero. The remaining prefix can be associated with a particular manufacturer (using data not included in PostgreSQL ). Sets the last 5 bytes of the address to zero. The remaining prefix can be associated with a particular manufacturer (using data not included in PostgreSQL ).

name: macaddr8_set7bit ( macaddr8 ) → macaddr8
describe: Sets the 7th bit of the address to one, creating what is known as modified EUI-64, for inclusion in an IPv6 address.

name: array_to_tsvector ( text[] ) → tsvector
describe: Converts an array of text strings to a tsvector . The given strings are used as lexemes as-is, without further processing. Array elements must not be empty strings or NULL .

name: get_current_ts_config ( ) → regconfig
describe: Returns the OID of the current default text search configuration (as set by default_text_search_config ).

name: length ( tsvector ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of lexemes in the tsvector .

name: numnode ( tsquery ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of lexemes plus operators in the tsquery .

name: plainto_tsquery ( [ config regconfig , ] query text ) → tsquery
describe: Converts text to a tsquery , normalizing words according to the specified or default configuration. Any punctuation in the string is ignored (it does not determine query operators). The resulting query matches documents containing all non-stopwords in the text.

name: phraseto_tsquery ( [ config regconfig , ] query text ) → tsquery
describe: Converts text to a tsquery , normalizing words according to the specified or default configuration. Any punctuation in the string is ignored (it does not determine query operators). The resulting query matches phrases containing all non-stopwords in the text.
example:
phraseto_tsquery('english', 'The Fat Rats') → 'fat' <-> 'rat'
phraseto_tsquery('english', 'The Cat and Rats') → 'cat' <2> 'rat'

name: websearch_to_tsquery ( [ config regconfig , ] query text ) → tsquery
describe: Converts text to a tsquery , normalizing words according to the specified or default configuration. Quoted word sequences are converted to phrase tests. The word “ or ” is understood as producing an OR operator, and a dash produces a NOT operator; other punctuation is ignored. This approximates the behavior of some common web search tools.

name: querytree ( tsquery ) → text
describe: Produces a representation of the indexable portion of a tsquery . A result that is empty or just T indicates a non-indexable query.

name:
setweight ( vector tsvector , weight "char" ) → tsvector
setweight ( vector tsvector , weight "char" , lexemes text[] ) → tsvector
describe: Assigns the specified weight to each element of the vector . Assigns the specified weight to elements of the vector that are listed in lexemes . The strings in lexemes are taken as lexemes as-is, without further processing. Strings that do not match any lexeme in vector are ignored.
example:
setweight('fat:2,4 cat:3 rat:5B'::tsvector, 'A') → 'cat':3A 'fat':2A,4A 'rat':5A
setweight('fat:2,4 cat:3 rat:5,6B'::tsvector, 'A', '{cat,rat}') → 'cat':3A 'fat':2,4 'rat':5A,6A

name: strip ( tsvector ) → tsvector
describe: Removes positions and weights from the tsvector .

name: to_tsquery ( [ config regconfig , ] query text ) → tsquery
describe: Converts text to a tsquery , normalizing words according to the specified or default configuration. The words must be combined by valid tsquery operators.

name:
to_tsvector ( [ config regconfig , ] document text ) → tsvector
to_tsvector ( [ config regconfig , ] document json ) → tsvector
to_tsvector ( [ config regconfig , ] document jsonb ) → tsvector
describe: Converts text to a tsvector , normalizing words according to the specified or default configuration. Position information is included in the result. Converts each string value in the JSON document to a tsvector , normalizing words according to the specified or default configuration. The results are then concatenated in document order to produce the output. Position information is generated as though one stopword exists between each pair of string values. (Beware that “ document order ” of the fields of a JSON object is implementation-dependent when the input is jsonb ; observe the difference in the examples.)
example:
to_tsvector('english', 'The Fat Rats') → 'fat':2 'rat':3
to_tsvector('english', '{"aa": "The Fat Rats", "b": "dog"}'::json) → 'dog':5 'fat':2 'rat':3
to_tsvector('english', '{"aa": "The Fat Rats", "b": "dog"}'::jsonb) → 'dog':1 'fat':4 'rat':5

name:
json_to_tsvector ( [ config regconfig , ] document json , filter jsonb ) → tsvector
jsonb_to_tsvector ( [ config regconfig , ] document jsonb , filter jsonb ) → tsvector
describe: Selects each item in the JSON document that is requested by the filter and converts each one to a tsvector , normalizing words according to the specified or default configuration. The results are then concatenated in document order to produce the output. Position information is generated as though one stopword exists between each pair of selected items. (Beware that “ document order ” of the fields of a JSON object is implementation-dependent when the input is jsonb .) The filter must be a jsonb array containing zero or more of these keywords: "string" (to include all string values), "numeric" (to include all numeric values), "boolean" (to include all boolean values), "key" (to include all keys), or "all" (to include all the above). As a special case, the filter can also be a simple JSON value that is one of these keywords.
example:
json_to_tsvector('english', '{"a": "The Fat Rats", "b": 123}'::json, '["string", "numeric"]') → '123':5 'fat':2 'rat':3
json_to_tsvector('english', '{"cat": "The Fat Rats", "dog": 123}'::json, '"all"') → '123':9 'cat':1 'dog':7 'fat':4 'rat':5

name:
ts_delete ( vector tsvector , lexeme text ) → tsvector
ts_delete ( vector tsvector , lexemes text[] ) → tsvector
describe: Removes any occurrence of the given lexeme from the vector . The lexeme string is treated as a lexeme as-is, without further processing. Removes any occurrences of the lexemes in lexemes from the vector . The strings in lexemes are taken as lexemes as-is, without further processing. Strings that do not match any lexeme in vector are ignored.
example:
ts_delete('fat:2,4 cat:3 rat:5A'::tsvector, 'fat') → 'cat':3 'rat':5A
ts_delete('fat:2,4 cat:3 rat:5A'::tsvector, ARRAY['fat','rat']) → 'cat':3

name: ts_filter ( vector tsvector , weights "char"[] ) → tsvector
describe: Selects only elements with the given weights from the vector .

name:
ts_headline ( [ config regconfig , ] document text , query tsquery [ , options text ] ) → text
ts_headline ( [ config regconfig , ] document json , query tsquery [ , options text ] ) → text
ts_headline ( [ config regconfig , ] document jsonb , query tsquery [ , options text ] ) → text
describe: Displays, in an abbreviated form, the match(es) for the query in the document , which must be raw text not a tsvector . Words in the document are normalized according to the specified or default configuration before matching to the query. Use of this function is discussed in Section 12.3.4 , which also describes the available options . Displays, in an abbreviated form, match(es) for the query that occur in string values within the JSON document . See Section 12.3.4 for more details.
example: ts_headline('The fat cat ate the rat.', 'cat') → The fat cat ate the rat. ts_headline('{"cat":"raining cats and dogs"}'::jsonb, 'cat') → {"cat": "raining cats and dogs"}

name: ts_rank ( [ weights real[] , ] vector tsvector , query tsquery [ , normalization integer ] ) → real
describe: Computes a score showing how well the vector matches the query . See Section 12.3.3 for details.

name: ts_rank_cd ( [ weights real[] , ] vector tsvector , query tsquery [ , normalization integer ] ) → real
describe: Computes a score showing how well the vector matches the query , using a cover density algorithm. See Section 12.3.3 for details.

name:
ts_rewrite ( query tsquery , target tsquery , substitute tsquery ) → tsquery
ts_rewrite ( query tsquery , select text ) → tsquery
describe: Replaces occurrences of target with substitute within the query . See Section 12.4.2.1 for details. Replaces portions of the query according to target(s) and substitute(s) obtained by executing a SELECT command. See Section 12.4.2.1 for details.
example:
ts_rewrite('a & b'::tsquery, 'a'::tsquery, 'foo|bar'::tsquery) → 'b' & ( 'foo' | 'bar' )
SELECT ts_rewrite('a & b'::tsquery, 'SELECT t,s FROM aliases') → 'b' & ( 'foo' | 'bar' )

name:
tsquery_phrase ( query1 tsquery , query2 tsquery ) → tsquery
tsquery_phrase ( query1 tsquery , query2 tsquery , distance integer ) → tsquery
describe: Constructs a phrase query that searches for matches of query1 and query2 at successive lexemes (same as <-> operator). Constructs a phrase query that searches for matches of query1 and query2 that occur exactly distance lexemes apart.

name: tsvector_to_array ( tsvector ) → text[]
describe: Converts a tsvector to an array of lexemes.

name: unnest ( tsvector ) → setof record ( lexeme text , positions smallint[] , weights text )
describe: Expands a tsvector into a set of rows, one per lexeme.

name: ts_debug ( [ config regconfig , ] document text ) → setof record ( alias text , description text , token text , dictionaries regdictionary[] , dictionary regdictionary , lexemes text[] )
describe: Extracts and normalizes tokens from the document according to the specified or default text search configuration, and returns information about how each token was processed. See Section 12.8.1 for details.

name: ts_lexize ( dict regdictionary , token text ) → text[]
describe: Returns an array of replacement lexemes if the input token is known to the dictionary, or an empty array if the token is known to the dictionary but it is a stop word, or NULL if it is not a known word. See Section 12.8.3 for details.

name:
ts_parse ( parser_name text , document text ) → setof record ( tokid integer , token text )
ts_parse ( parser_oid oid , document text ) → setof record ( tokid integer , token text )
describe: Extracts tokens from the document using the named parser. See Section 12.8.2 for details. Extracts tokens from the document using a parser specified by OID. See Section 12.8.2 for details.

name:
ts_token_type ( parser_name text ) → setof record ( tokid integer , alias text , description text )
ts_token_type ( parser_oid oid ) → setof record ( tokid integer , alias text , description text )
describe: Returns a table that describes each type of token the named parser can recognize. See Section 12.8.2 for details. Returns a table that describes each type of token a parser specified by OID can recognize. See Section 12.8.2 for details.
example:
ts_token_type('default') → (1,asciiword,"Word, all ASCII") ...
ts_token_type(3722) → (1,asciiword,"Word, all ASCII") ...

name: ts_stat ( sqlquery text [ , weights text ] ) → setof record ( word text , ndoc integer , nentry integer )
describe: Executes the sqlquery , which must return a single tsvector column, and returns statistics about each distinct lexeme contained in the data. See Section 12.4.4 for details.

name:
to_json ( anyelement ) → json
to_jsonb ( anyelement ) → jsonb
describe: Converts any SQL value to json or jsonb . Arrays and composites are converted recursively to arrays and objects (multidimensional arrays become arrays of arrays in JSON). Otherwise, if there is a cast from the SQL data type to json , the cast function will be used to perform the conversion; [a] otherwise, a scalar JSON value is produced. For any scalar other than a number, a Boolean, or a null value, the text representation will be used, with escaping as necessary to make it a valid JSON string value.
example:
to_json('Fred said "Hi."'::text) → "Fred said \"Hi.\""
to_jsonb(row(42, 'Fred said "Hi."'::text)) → {"f1": 42, "f2": "Fred said \"Hi.\""}

name: array_to_json ( anyarray [ , boolean ] ) → json
describe: Converts an SQL array to a JSON array. The behavior is the same as to_json except that line feeds will be added between top-level array elements if the optional boolean parameter is true.

name:
json_array ( [ { value_expression [ FORMAT JSON ] } [ , ... ] ] [ { NULL | ABSENT } ON NULL ] [ RETURNING data_type [ FORMAT JSON [ ENCODING UTF8 ] ] ])
json_array ( [ query_expression ] [ RETURNING data_type [ FORMAT JSON [ ENCODING UTF8 ] ] ])
describe: Constructs a JSON array from either a series of value_expression parameters or from the results of query_expression , which must be a SELECT query returning a single column. If ABSENT ON NULL is specified, NULL values are ignored. This is always the case if a query_expression is used.

name: row_to_json ( record [ , boolean ] ) → json
describe: Converts an SQL composite value to a JSON object. The behavior is the same as to_json except that line feeds will be added between top-level elements if the optional boolean parameter is true.

name:
json_build_array ( VARIADIC "any" ) → json
jsonb_build_array ( VARIADIC "any" ) → jsonb
describe: Builds a possibly-heterogeneously-typed JSON array out of a variadic argument list. Each argument is converted as per to_json or to_jsonb .

name:
json_build_object ( VARIADIC "any" ) → json
jsonb_build_object ( VARIADIC "any" ) → jsonb
describe: Builds a JSON object out of a variadic argument list. By convention, the argument list consists of alternating keys and values. Key arguments are coerced to text; value arguments are converted as per to_json or to_jsonb .

name:
json_object ( [ { key_expression { VALUE | ':' } value_expression [ FORMAT JSON [ ENCODING UTF8 ] ] }[ , ... ] ] [ { NULL | ABSENT } ON NULL ] [ { WITH | WITHOUT } UNIQUE [ KEYS ] ] [ RETURNING data_type [ FORMAT JSON [ ENCODING UTF8 ] ] ])
json_object ( text[] ) → json
jsonb_object ( text[] ) → jsonb
json_object ( keys text[] , values text[] ) → json
jsonb_object ( keys text[] , values text[] ) → jsonb
describe: Constructs a JSON object of all the key/value pairs given, or an empty object if none are given. key_expression is a scalar expression defining the JSON key, which is converted to the text type. It cannot be NULL nor can it belong to a type that has a cast to the json type. If WITH UNIQUE KEYS is specified, there must not be any duplicate key_expression . Any pair for which the value_expression evaluates to NULL is omitted from the output if ABSENT ON NULL is specified; if NULL ON NULL is specified or the clause omitted, the key is included with value NULL . Builds a JSON object out of a text array. The array must have either exactly one dimension with an even number of members, in which case they are taken as alternating key/value pairs, or two dimensions such that each inner array has exactly two elements, which are taken as a key/value pair. All values are converted to JSON strings. This form of json_object takes keys and values pairwise from separate text arrays. Otherwise it is identical to the one-argument form.

name: json ( expression [ FORMAT JSON [ ENCODING UTF8 ] ] [ { WITH | WITHOUT } UNIQUE [ KEYS ] ] ) → json
describe: Converts a given expression specified as text or bytea string (in UTF8 encoding) into a JSON value. If expression is NULL, an SQL null value is returned. If WITH UNIQUE is specified, the expression must not contain any duplicate object keys.

name: json_scalar ( expression )
describe: Converts a given SQL scalar value into a JSON scalar value. If the input is NULL, an SQL null is returned. If the input is number or a boolean value, a corresponding JSON number or boolean value is returned. For any other value, a JSON string is returned.

name: json_serialize ( expression [ FORMAT JSON [ ENCODING UTF8 ] ] [ RETURNING data_type [ FORMAT JSON [ ENCODING UTF8 ] ] ] )
describe: Converts an SQL/JSON expression into a character or binary string. The expression can be of any JSON type, any character string type, or bytea in UTF8 encoding. The returned type used in RETURNING can be any character string type or bytea . The default is text .

name:
json_array_elements ( json ) → setof json
jsonb_array_elements ( jsonb ) → setof jsonb
describe: Expands the top-level JSON array into a set of JSON values.

name:
json_array_elements_text ( json ) → setof text
jsonb_array_elements_text ( jsonb ) → setof text
describe: Expands the top-level JSON array into a set of text values.

name:
json_array_length ( json ) → integer
jsonb_array_length ( jsonb ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of elements in the top-level JSON array.

name:
json_each ( json ) → setof record ( key text , value json )
jsonb_each ( jsonb ) → setof record ( key text , value jsonb )
describe: Expands the top-level JSON object into a set of key/value pairs.

name:
json_each_text ( json ) → setof record ( key text , value text )
jsonb_each_text ( jsonb ) → setof record ( key text , value text )
describe: Expands the top-level JSON object into a set of key/value pairs. The returned value s will be of type text .

name:
json_extract_path ( from_json json , VARIADIC path_elems text[] ) → json
jsonb_extract_path ( from_json jsonb , VARIADIC path_elems text[] ) → jsonb
describe: Extracts JSON sub-object at the specified path. (This is functionally equivalent to the #> operator, but writing the path out as a variadic list can be more convenient in some cases.)

name:
json_extract_path_text ( from_json json , VARIADIC path_elems text[] ) → text
jsonb_extract_path_text ( from_json jsonb , VARIADIC path_elems text[] ) → text
describe: Extracts JSON sub-object at the specified path as text . (This is functionally equivalent to the #>> operator.)

name:
json_object_keys ( json ) → setof text
jsonb_object_keys ( jsonb ) → setof text
describe: Returns the set of keys in the top-level JSON object.

name:
json_populate_record ( base anyelement , from_json json ) → anyelement
jsonb_populate_record ( base anyelement , from_json jsonb ) → anyelement
describe: Expands the top-level JSON object to a row having the composite type of the base argument. The JSON object is scanned for fields whose names match column names of the output row type, and their values are inserted into those columns of the output. (Fields that do not correspond to any output column name are ignored.) In typical use, the value of base is just NULL , which means that any output columns that do not match any object field will be filled with nulls. However, if base isn't NULL then the values it contains will be used for unmatched columns. To convert a JSON value to the SQL type of an output column, the following rules are applied in sequence: A JSON null value is converted to an SQL null in all cases. If the output column is of type json or jsonb , the JSON value is just reproduced exactly. If the output column is a composite (row) type, and the JSON value is a JSON object, the fields of the object are converted to columns of the output row type by recursive application of these rules. Likewise, if the output column is an array type and the JSON value is a JSON array, the elements of the JSON array are converted to elements of the output array by recursive application of these rules. Otherwise, if the JSON value is a string, the contents of the string are fed to the input conversion function for the column's data type. Otherwise, the ordinary text representation of the JSON value is fed to the input conversion function for the column's data type. While the example below uses a constant JSON value, typical use would be to reference a json or jsonb column laterally from another table in the query's FROM clause. Writing json_populate_record in the FROM clause is good practice, since all of the extracted columns are available for use without duplicate function calls. create type subrowtype as (d int, e text); create type myrowtype as (a int, b text[], c subrowtype);
example: select * from json_populate_record(null::myrowtype, '{"a": 1, "b": ["2", "a b"], "c": {"d": 4, "e": "a b c"}, "x": "foo"}') →

name: jsonb_populate_record_valid ( base anyelement , from_json json ) → boolean
describe: Function for testing jsonb_populate_record . Returns true if the input jsonb_populate_record would finish without an error for the given input JSON object; that is, it's valid input, false otherwise. create type jsb_char2 as (a char(2));
example:
select jsonb_populate_record_valid(NULL::jsb_char2, '{"a": "aaa"}'); →
select * from jsonb_populate_record(NULL::jsb_char2, '{"a": "aaa"}') q; →
select jsonb_populate_record_valid(NULL::jsb_char2, '{"a": "aa"}'); →
select * from jsonb_populate_record(NULL::jsb_char2, '{"a": "aa"}') q; →

name:
json_populate_recordset ( base anyelement , from_json json ) → setof anyelement
jsonb_populate_recordset ( base anyelement , from_json jsonb ) → setof anyelement
describe: Expands the top-level JSON array of objects to a set of rows having the composite type of the base argument. Each element of the JSON array is processed as described above for json[b]_populate_record . create type twoints as (a int, b int);

name:
json_to_record ( json ) → record
jsonb_to_record ( jsonb ) → record
describe: Expands the top-level JSON object to a row having the composite type defined by an AS clause. (As with all functions returning record , the calling query must explicitly define the structure of the record with an AS clause.) The output record is filled from fields of the JSON object, in the same way as described above for json[b]_populate_record . Since there is no input record value, unmatched columns are always filled with nulls. create type myrowtype as (a int, b text);
example: select * from json_to_record('{"a":1,"b":[1,2,3],"c":[1,2,3],"e":"bar","r": {"a": 123, "b": "a b c"}}') as x(a int, b text, c int[], d text, r myrowtype) →

name:
json_to_recordset ( json ) → setof record
jsonb_to_recordset ( jsonb ) → setof record
describe: Expands the top-level JSON array of objects to a set of rows having the composite type defined by an AS clause. (As with all functions returning record , the calling query must explicitly define the structure of the record with an AS clause.) Each element of the JSON array is processed as described above for json[b]_populate_record .
example: select * from json_to_recordset('[{"a":1,"b":"foo"}, {"a":"2","c":"bar"}]') as x(a int, b text) →

name: jsonb_set ( target jsonb , path text[] , new_value jsonb [ , create_if_missing boolean ] ) → jsonb
describe: Returns target with the item designated by path replaced by new_value , or with new_value added if create_if_missing is true (which is the default) and the item designated by path does not exist. All earlier steps in the path must exist, or the target is returned unchanged. As with the path oriented operators, negative integers that appear in the path count from the end of JSON arrays. If the last path step is an array index that is out of range, and create_if_missing is true, the new value is added at the beginning of the array if the index is negative, or at the end of the array if it is positive.

name: jsonb_set_lax ( target jsonb , path text[] , new_value jsonb [ , create_if_missing boolean [ , null_value_treatment text ] ] ) → jsonb
describe: If new_value is not NULL , behaves identically to jsonb_set . Otherwise behaves according to the value of null_value_treatment which must be one of 'raise_exception' , 'use_json_null' , 'delete_key' , or 'return_target' . The default is 'use_json_null' .
example:
jsonb_set_lax('[{"f1":1,"f2":null},2,null,3]', '{0,f1}', null) → [{"f1": null, "f2": null}, 2, null, 3]
jsonb_set_lax('[{"f1":99,"f2":null},2]', '{0,f3}', null, true, 'return_target') → [{"f1": 99, "f2": null}, 2]

name: jsonb_insert ( target jsonb , path text[] , new_value jsonb [ , insert_after boolean ] ) → jsonb
describe: Returns target with new_value inserted. If the item designated by the path is an array element, new_value will be inserted before that item if insert_after is false (which is the default), or after it if insert_after is true. If the item designated by the path is an object field, new_value will be inserted only if the object does not already contain that key. All earlier steps in the path must exist, or the target is returned unchanged. As with the path oriented operators, negative integers that appear in the path count from the end of JSON arrays. If the last path step is an array index that is out of range, the new value is added at the beginning of the array if the index is negative, or at the end of the array if it is positive.

name:
json_strip_nulls ( json ) → json
jsonb_strip_nulls ( jsonb ) → jsonb
describe: Deletes all object fields that have null values from the given JSON value, recursively. Null values that are not object fields are untouched.

name: jsonb_path_exists ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → boolean
describe: Checks whether the JSON path returns any item for the specified JSON value. (This is useful only with SQL-standard JSON path expressions, not predicate check expressions , since those always return a value.) If the vars argument is specified, it must be a JSON object, and its fields provide named values to be substituted into the jsonpath expression. If the silent argument is specified and is true , the function suppresses the same errors as the @? and @@ operators do.

name: jsonb_path_match ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → boolean
describe: Returns the SQL boolean result of a JSON path predicate check for the specified JSON value. (This is useful only with predicate check expressions , not SQL-standard JSON path expressions, since it will either fail or return NULL if the path result is not a single boolean value.) The optional vars and silent arguments act the same as for jsonb_path_exists .

name: jsonb_path_query ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → setof jsonb
describe: Returns all JSON items returned by the JSON path for the specified JSON value. For SQL-standard JSON path expressions it returns the JSON values selected from target . For predicate check expressions it returns the result of the predicate check: true , false , or null . The optional vars and silent arguments act the same as for jsonb_path_exists .

name: jsonb_path_query_array ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → jsonb
describe: Returns all JSON items returned by the JSON path for the specified JSON value, as a JSON array. The parameters are the same as for jsonb_path_query .

name: jsonb_path_query_first ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → jsonb
describe: Returns the first JSON item returned by the JSON path for the specified JSON value, or NULL if there are no results. The parameters are the same as for jsonb_path_query .

name:
jsonb_path_exists_tz ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → boolean
jsonb_path_match_tz ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → boolean
jsonb_path_query_tz ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → setof jsonb
jsonb_path_query_array_tz ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → jsonb
jsonb_path_query_first_tz ( target jsonb , path jsonpath [ , vars jsonb [ , silent boolean ] ] ) → jsonb
describe: These functions act like their counterparts described above without the _tz suffix, except that these functions support comparisons of date/time values that require timezone-aware conversions. The example below requires interpretation of the date-only value 2015-08-02 as a timestamp with time zone, so the result depends on the current TimeZone setting. Due to this dependency, these functions are marked as stable, which means these functions cannot be used in indexes. Their counterparts are immutable, and so can be used in indexes; but they will throw errors if asked to make such comparisons.

name: jsonb_pretty ( jsonb ) → text
describe: Converts the given JSON value to pretty-printed, indented text.

name:
json_typeof ( json ) → text
jsonb_typeof ( jsonb ) → text
describe: Returns the type of the top-level JSON value as a text string. Possible types are object , array , string , number , boolean , and null . (The null result should not be confused with an SQL NULL; see the examples.)
example:
json_typeof('-123.4') → number
json_typeof('null'::json) → null
json_typeof(NULL::json) IS NULL → t

name: nextval ( regclass ) → bigint
describe: Advances the sequence object to its next value and returns that value. This is done atomically: even if multiple sessions execute nextval concurrently, each will safely receive a distinct sequence value. If the sequence object has been created with default parameters, successive nextval calls will return successive values beginning with 1. Other behaviors can be obtained by using appropriate parameters in the CREATE SEQUENCE command. This function requires USAGE or UPDATE privilege on the sequence.

name: setval ( regclass , bigint [ , boolean ] ) → bigint
describe: Sets the sequence object's current value, and optionally its is_called flag. The two-parameter form sets the sequence's last_value field to the specified value and sets its is_called field to true , meaning that the next nextval will advance the sequence before returning a value. The value that will be reported by currval is also set to the specified value. In the three-parameter form, is_called can be set to either true or false . true has the same effect as the two-parameter form. If it is set to false , the next nextval will return exactly the specified value, and sequence advancement commences with the following nextval . Furthermore, the value reported by currval is not changed in this case. For example, The result returned by setval is just the value of its second argument. This function requires UPDATE privilege on the sequence.

name: currval ( regclass ) → bigint
describe: Returns the value most recently obtained by nextval for this sequence in the current session. (An error is reported if nextval has never been called for this sequence in this session.) Because this is returning a session-local value, it gives a predictable answer whether or not other sessions have executed nextval since the current session did. This function requires USAGE or SELECT privilege on the sequence.

name: lastval () → bigint
describe: Returns the value most recently returned by nextval in the current session. This function is identical to currval , except that instead of taking the sequence name as an argument it refers to whichever sequence nextval was most recently applied to in the current session. It is an error to call lastval if nextval has not yet been called in the current session. This function requires USAGE or SELECT privilege on the last used sequence.

name: array_append ( anycompatiblearray , anycompatible ) → anycompatiblearray
describe: Appends an element to the end of an array (same as the anycompatiblearray || anycompatible operator).

name: array_cat ( anycompatiblearray , anycompatiblearray ) → anycompatiblearray
describe: Concatenates two arrays (same as the anycompatiblearray || anycompatiblearray operator).

name: array_dims ( anyarray ) → text
describe: Returns a text representation of the array's dimensions.

name: array_fill ( anyelement , integer[] [ , integer[] ] ) → anyarray
describe: Returns an array filled with copies of the given value, having dimensions of the lengths specified by the second argument. The optional third argument supplies lower-bound values for each dimension (which default to all 1 ).

name: array_length ( anyarray , integer ) → integer
describe: Returns the length of the requested array dimension. (Produces NULL instead of 0 for empty or missing array dimensions.)

name: array_lower ( anyarray , integer ) → integer
describe: Returns the lower bound of the requested array dimension.

name: array_ndims ( anyarray ) → integer
describe: Returns the number of dimensions of the array.

name: array_position ( anycompatiblearray , anycompatible [ , integer ] ) → integer
describe: Returns the subscript of the first occurrence of the second argument in the array, or NULL if it's not present. If the third argument is given, the search begins at that subscript. The array must be one-dimensional. Comparisons are done using IS NOT DISTINCT FROM semantics, so it is possible to search for NULL .

name: array_positions ( anycompatiblearray , anycompatible ) → integer[]
describe: Returns an array of the subscripts of all occurrences of the second argument in the array given as first argument. The array must be one-dimensional. Comparisons are done using IS NOT DISTINCT FROM semantics, so it is possible to search for NULL . NULL is returned only if the array is NULL ; if the value is not found in the array, an empty array is returned.

name: array_prepend ( anycompatible , anycompatiblearray ) → anycompatiblearray
describe: Prepends an element to the beginning of an array (same as the anycompatible || anycompatiblearray operator).

name: array_remove ( anycompatiblearray , anycompatible ) → anycompatiblearray
describe: Removes all elements equal to the given value from the array. The array must be one-dimensional. Comparisons are done using IS NOT DISTINCT FROM semantics, so it is possible to remove NULL s.

name: array_replace ( anycompatiblearray , anycompatible , anycompatible ) → anycompatiblearray
describe: Replaces each array element equal to the second argument with the third argument.

name: array_sample ( array anyarray , n integer ) → anyarray
describe: Returns an array of n items randomly selected from array . n may not exceed the length of array 's first dimension. If array is multi-dimensional, an “ item ” is a slice having a given first subscript.

name: array_shuffle ( anyarray ) → anyarray
describe: Randomly shuffles the first dimension of the array.

name: array_to_string ( array anyarray , delimiter text [ , null_string text ] ) → text
describe: Converts each array element to its text representation, and concatenates those separated by the delimiter string. If null_string is given and is not NULL , then NULL array entries are represented by that string; otherwise, they are omitted. See also string_to_array .

name: array_upper ( anyarray , integer ) → integer
describe: Returns the upper bound of the requested array dimension.

name: cardinality ( anyarray ) → integer
describe: Returns the total number of elements in the array, or 0 if the array is empty.

name: trim_array ( array anyarray , n integer ) → anyarray
describe: Trims an array by removing the last n elements. If the array is multidimensional, only the first dimension is trimmed.

name:
unnest ( anyarray ) → setof anyelement
unnest ( anyarray , anyarray [ , ... ] ) → setof anyelement, anyelement [, ... ]
describe: Expands an array into a set of rows. The array's elements are read out in storage order. Expands multiple arrays (possibly of different data types) into a set of rows. If the arrays are not all the same length then the shorter ones are padded with NULL s. This form is only allowed in a query's FROM clause; see Section 7.2.1.4 .
example:
unnest(ARRAY[1,2]) →
unnest(ARRAY[['foo','bar'],['baz','quux']]) →
select * from unnest(ARRAY[1,2], ARRAY['foo','bar','baz']) as x(a,b) →

name:
lower ( anyrange ) → anyelement
lower ( anymultirange ) → anyelement
describe: Extracts the lower bound of the range ( NULL if the range is empty or has no lower bound). Extracts the lower bound of the multirange ( NULL if the multirange is empty has no lower bound).

name:
upper ( anyrange ) → anyelement
upper ( anymultirange ) → anyelement
describe: Extracts the upper bound of the range ( NULL if the range is empty or has no upper bound). Extracts the upper bound of the multirange ( NULL if the multirange is empty or has no upper bound).

name:
isempty ( anyrange ) → boolean
isempty ( anymultirange ) → boolean
describe: Is the range empty? Is the multirange empty?

name:
lower_inc ( anyrange ) → boolean
lower_inc ( anymultirange ) → boolean
describe: Is the range's lower bound inclusive? Is the multirange's lower bound inclusive?

name:
upper_inc ( anyrange ) → boolean
upper_inc ( anymultirange ) → boolean
describe: Is the range's upper bound inclusive? Is the multirange's upper bound inclusive?

name:
lower_inf ( anyrange ) → boolean
lower_inf ( anymultirange ) → boolean
describe: Does the range have no lower bound? (A lower bound of -Infinity returns false.) Does the multirange have no lower bound? (A lower bound of -Infinity returns false.)

name:
upper_inf ( anyrange ) → boolean
upper_inf ( anymultirange ) → boolean
describe: Does the range have no upper bound? (An upper bound of Infinity returns false.) Does the multirange have no upper bound? (An upper bound of Infinity returns false.)

name:
range_merge ( anyrange , anyrange ) → anyrange
range_merge ( anymultirange ) → anyrange
describe: Computes the smallest range that includes both of the given ranges. Computes the smallest range that includes the entire multirange.

name: multirange ( anyrange ) → anymultirange
describe: Returns a multirange containing just the given range.

name: unnest ( anymultirange ) → setof anyrange
describe: Expands a multirange into a set of ranges in ascending order.

name: any_value ( anyelement ) → same as input type
describe: Returns an arbitrary value from the non-null input values.

name:
array_agg ( anynonarray ORDER BY input_sort_columns ) → anyarray
array_agg ( anyarray ORDER BY input_sort_columns ) → anyarray
describe: Collects all the input values, including nulls, into an array. Concatenates all the input arrays into an array of one higher dimension. (The inputs must all have the same dimensionality, and cannot be empty or null.)

name:
avg ( smallint ) → numeric
avg ( integer ) → numeric
avg ( bigint ) → numeric
avg ( numeric ) → numeric
avg ( real ) → double precision
avg ( double precision ) → double precision
avg ( interval ) → interval
describe: Computes the average (arithmetic mean) of all the non-null input values.

name:
bit_and ( smallint ) → smallint
bit_and ( integer ) → integer
bit_and ( bigint ) → bigint
bit_and ( bit ) → bit
describe: Computes the bitwise AND of all non-null input values.

name:
bit_or ( smallint ) → smallint
bit_or ( integer ) → integer
bit_or ( bigint ) → bigint
bit_or ( bit ) → bit
describe: Computes the bitwise OR of all non-null input values.

name:
bit_xor ( smallint ) → smallint
bit_xor ( integer ) → integer
bit_xor ( bigint ) → bigint
bit_xor ( bit ) → bit
describe: Computes the bitwise exclusive OR of all non-null input values. Can be useful as a checksum for an unordered set of values.

name: bool_and ( boolean ) → boolean
describe: Returns true if all non-null input values are true, otherwise false.

name: bool_or ( boolean ) → boolean
